Notes: Post-2006 coup. Abhisit installed by courts and military. Military IS aligned with government — no civilian-military divide.
Abhisit government continues with full military backing. No friction between civilian and military authority.
Military deployed against Red Shirts Apr-May 2010 on civilian order — military serving government, not defying it. No civil-military friction.
Yingluck wins landslide July 2011. Military accepts result — unusual. Minor institutional tension as military adjusts to elected civilian government. No overt friction events.
Yingluck governs. Military quiet. No notable friction events recorded.
PDRC protests begin Nov 2013. Military begins signaling against Yingluck government. Constitutional amendment attempt blocked. Military publicly signals non-neutrality.
May 20 martial law declared. May 22 coup. General Prayuth removes elected civilian government. Maximum civil-military friction event — entire civilian government displaced.
NCPO junta IS the government. No civilian-military friction — all authority unified under military leadership. Civilian technocrats appointed by military.
Same as 2015. Military runs government directly. No friction.
New constitution approved. Military structures its own political continuation. No civilian friction.
NCPO continues. No meaningful civilian government to create friction against.
Elections held Mar 2019. Pheu Thai wins seats but Prayuth returned as PM via military-appointed 250-senator bloc. Civilian election results effectively overridden by military-designed constitution.

Future Forward Party dissolved Feb 2020. Prayuth government uses courts and security forces against civilian pro-democracy protesters (Jul-Nov 2020). Civilian democratic actors systematically suppressed.
Largest protest crackdowns. 259 individuals eventually charged. Military and civilian PM (Prayuth) aligned against pro-democracy movement. Coalition civilian members show occasional dissent.
Prayuth suspended by Constitutional Court Aug 2022 (8-year term limit case). Returned Sep after favorable ruling. Coalition civilian partners show open tension with military-aligned bloc.
MFP wins election May 2023 but blocked from forming government. New civilian Defence Minister Sutin Klungsang installed under Srettha. Sutin insists military reform underway amid military resistance.

Key_Event_Type: Military Reform Blocked — Rogue Army Editorial
Notes: Military reform bill (National Defence Organisation Act) withdrawn Dec 2024 after Bhumjaithai and UTN coalition partners reject it. Khaosod editorial: civilian government openly admits it cannot control Defence Ministry. ISEAS Paul Chambers: 2024 reshuffles structured as guardian tactic against civilian control.

Key_Event_Type: Army Commander Defies PM — Border Crisis
Notes: May: Gen. Boonsin unilaterally closes border defying PM. Jun 15: Paetongtarn tells Hun Sen she cannot control Boonsin — royal entourage visits Boonsin simultaneously. Jul 16-20: Boonsin declares no government order needed for tactical responses. Aug 12: Khaosod analysis — civilian government relegated to secondary role. Aug 29: Paetongtarn removed after Hun Sen call crisis. Dec: Military leverages border crisis to reassert political dominance.

Anutin Charnvirakul as PM (~21% approval). Military continues to operate with high autonomy in border zone. Civilian government lacks authority to direct military operations. Friction persists but below 2025 peak.
